name: NodeOps
draft_rationale: NodeOps sells a DePIN compute-orchestration and one-click node/app deployment platform (CreateOS) built around blockchain compute, managed infrastructure, and a builder marketplace. The 'AI-powered' tagline and 'AI-assisted creation/deployment' comparison rows are bolt-on conveniences; the core mechanism is decentralized compute and PaaS deployment, which survives untouched if all AI is removed. That makes it traditional infrastructure software, not AI-native.
ambiguity_flag: Could be argued 0B if the 'AI-assisted' editor/deploy features are genuine shipped functionality rather than marketing, but the AI is peripheral to a compute/deployment platform.

name: Intelligems
draft_rationale: Intelligems is a Shopify A/B testing, pricing, and personalization platform whose core mechanism is experimentation and analytics for e-commerce profit optimization. AI appears only as one 'AI-powered optimization tools' item among 50+ product updates, so removing AI leaves the testing/analytics product fully intact. This is conventional SaaS.

name: GibsonAI
draft_rationale: The Crunchbase copy describes an 'AI database engineer,' but the Website Evidence shows a pivot to Memori: agent-native memory infrastructure (an LLM-agnostic layer that captures, structures, and recalls memory for LLM apps and agents). The customer is an AI developer and the product exists only because of the LLM/agent ecosystem, which is 1B. RAD-M rather than RAD-H because the evidence cites tailored/proprietary memory models, a memory-graph architecture, and benchmark leadership (LoCoMo) that show real proprietary depth beyond a bare API call.
ambiguity_flag: Pivot from the Crunchbase description noted; 1D is the close alternative if the memory graph/tailored models are read as a thick integrator rather than developer infrastructure.

name: Cynthia.io
draft_rationale: Cynthia.io's evidence is almost entirely slogans ('cognitive search driven by her deep neural networks,' 'custom AI solutions on demand') with about/features/pricing pages all empty and no product mechanism shown. Under the marketing-vs-mechanism guardrail and the coin-flip-goes-to-0 rule, unsupported AI branding with no demonstrated mechanism does not qualify as AI-native, so I classify the presented software (a search product) as 0A. Confidence is low given how thin the evidence is.
ambiguity_flag: If the neural/semantic search engine is genuinely ML-driven it would be 1E/1D, and if it is really an on-demand AI services shop it would be 0C; the evidence is too marketing-only to resolve.

name: Sierra Grande School District
draft_rationale: Sierra Grande School District is a public K-12 school district; the evidence is entirely school portals, spotlights, employment links, and district finance pages. It sells no software and no AI product, it delivers education services, which is 0C.

name: OneImaging
draft_rationale: OneImaging is a radiology-savings/care-navigation benefit: it books imaging at a national network of centers, shows upfront prices, and pairs members with human care navigators, an employer-sponsored services business. The 'AI decision support' is explicitly future ('Next up'), so it is not a shipped mechanism. The deliverable is a service (network access, negotiated pricing, concierge), which is 0C.
ambiguity_flag: 0A is plausible because there is a booking app/platform, but the core value is a healthcare service rather than a software product.

name: Integrity Bank for Business
draft_rationale: Integrity Bank for Business is a community bank offering commercial and personal banking, lending, and cash-management services. The evidence is relationship-banking testimonials and account/loan descriptions with no software product and no AI. A financial-services business delivered through an ordinary website is 0C.

name: ClevopyAI
draft_rationale: ClevopyAI is an AI marketing-copywriting tool: 90+ templates that generate blog posts, ads, product descriptions, and social copy, plus an image generator, all produced by LLM calls. The taxonomy explicitly puts simple AI writing assistants in 1C, and the evidence shows a tiny team, no proprietary data, no fine-tuning, and easy replicability. RAD-H follows from GENAI-era founding and full dependence on external model APIs with no proprietary depth.
ambiguity_flag: 1G is a close alternative because it also generates images, but the primary deliverable is generated marketing text, which the taxonomy treats as a thin writing-assistant wrapper (1C).

name: Chantico Technology
draft_rationale: Chantico's Caminos platform is investment/portfolio analytics: scenario modeling, risk monitoring, dashboards, and client narratives for portfolio managers, with a real data-science team and a 'data recommendation engine' plus 'machine and deep learning' claims. The core scenario-modeling and risk-visualization workflow could exist with conventional quant analytics, so ML augments rather than constitutes the product. That is the AI-augmented pattern, 0B.
ambiguity_flag: 1E is plausible if the deep-learning/recommendation engine is truly the core mechanism, and 0A if the ML is mostly branding; the evidence sits between them.

name: Cambrya
draft_rationale: Cambrya is building a novel, biologically-inspired computing paradigm: unsupervised 'learning by association' algorithms and a neuromorphic/analog architecture, first proven on a blind-source-separation 'artificial ear,' offered as one platform for many applications. The artifact is a new learning architecture that others would build on, which is foundation-layer AI research (1A). RAD-L because the whole approach is proprietary, self-developed, and structurally independent of third-party GenAI APIs (in fact it rejects big-data/brute-force approaches).
ambiguity_flag: Early-stage/research company; classification rests on novel-architecture research, and 1E is a weaker alternative if one reads it as vertical (audio/hearing) rather than a horizontal computing platform.

name: Every
draft_rationale: Every is an all-in-one startup back-office fintech: incorporation, banking, treasury, payroll/HR, bookkeeping, and tax prep. Despite the 'AI Native' slogan, the genuine AI is narrow ('AI-enriched account categorization,' 'AI to retrieve your EIN instantly') and the full financial suite plainly predates and survives without AI. That is conventional fintech with bolted-on AI features, which the guardrail assigns to 0B, not an AI-native subclass.
ambiguity_flag: 0A is a close call if the AI-enriched bookkeeping is judged too minor to count as a meaningful shipped feature.

name: Reflex Robotics
draft_rationale: Reflex Robotics builds affordable general-purpose humanoid robots for warehouse/factory work, explicitly following the Tesla-FSD playbook: deploy hardware, collect real-world data via a custom tele-op system, and iteratively ramp autonomy. The product is embodied applied AI where proprietary perception/control models trained on an in-house data flywheel are the core value, which is 1E. RAD-M because the autonomy models are proprietary and rooted in pre-GenAI robotics/CV ML, but the company is early-stage with autonomy still under development.
ambiguity_flag: 1F is the close alternative given the autonomy goal, but the product is currently human-in-the-loop teleoperation, so it is not yet autonomous multi-step execution; a 0B reading is possible if autonomy is seen as aspirational.

name: Electives
draft_rationale: Electives is an enterprise live-learning platform whose core is live, human-instructor-led classes plus L&D program management, which predates and survives without AI. It ships genuine AI features (AI role-play 'Simulations,' an AI coach, and AI-readiness training), so AI is a meaningful add-on to a pre-existing learning product, which is 0B.
ambiguity_flag: 0C is possible if judged primarily a live-training/instructor services business rather than a software platform (both remain RAD-NA).

name: LSA Autonomy
draft_rationale: LSA Autonomy (Mach) supplies full-stack off-road vehicle autonomy to OEMs: navigation and path planning, an Edge-AI stereo-vision perception platform for obstacle detection/classification, and patent-pending phased-array radar, commercially proven across 30+ vehicle platforms. The perception/planning AI is the core mechanism of a vertical autonomy product with strong proprietary depth, which is 1E. RAD-L because it builds and runs its own perception/autonomy models (pre-GenAI CV/robotics), with no dependence on third-party GenAI APIs.
ambiguity_flag: 1F is the close alternative since the vehicles act autonomously in the world; I chose 1E for the vertical, proprietary-CV robotics profile.

name: NowDraft
draft_rationale: NowDraft generates SEO blog articles and marketing copy from a single keyword/topic via LLMs, with Amazon/Shopify/Zapier hooks, a thin AI writing wrapper. Removing the LLM call collapses the product, and there is a tiny team with no proprietary data, fine-tuning, or workflow moat, which is 1C. RAD-H because it is structurally dependent on external model APIs with no proprietary depth despite the pre-March-2023 founding.

name: RobinApp.co
draft_rationale: RobinApp.co sells in-app chat APIs and SDKs (reactions, replies, presence, groups, read receipts) for developers adding messaging to apps, a conventional communication-infrastructure product. The recent 'AI-powered chat' page is a chat-UI component that lets developers wire in OpenAI/Claude/Gemini, a bolt-on feature on a pre-AI messaging SDK. Core value is unchanged without AI, so it is 0A.
ambiguity_flag: 0B is plausible if the AI chat-UI is treated as a meaningful shipped feature, and 1B if one over-weights the 'chat UI for AI' framing; the primary product is general messaging infrastructure.

name: Marquis Wealth Group
draft_rationale: Marquis Wealth Group is a registered investment advisory (RIA) firm selling financial planning and wealth-management advice under a fiduciary standard ('we sell advice, not products'). The evidence is advisor bios, process steps, and 529 resources, with no software product and no AI. A professional financial-advice services firm is 0C.

name: BiomEdit
draft_rationale: BiomEdit is an animal-health microbiome biotech that discovers, designs, and develops probiotics, microbiome-derived bioactives, and engineered microbial medicines using high-throughput sequencing and data analytics. Its saleable product is biological, not software, and the grant-funded 'Rumen Digital Twin' AI model is a discovery tool/open-access research project rather than the commercial product. A biotech selling biological goods is 0C.
ambiguity_flag: 1E is a genuine close call if the AI foundation-model/digital-twin platform is treated as the core product, and 0B if BiomEdit is read as a tech company using AI; I weighted the biological-product deliverable.

name: Cabinet
draft_rationale: Cabinet is a scheduling/calendar software platform for executive assistants: group-scheduling polls, booking links, shadow holds, meeting logs, and calendar analytics, integrated with Google/Outlook/Zoom. The 'intelligent'/'automation' language is light and the core scheduling toolset is a conventional productivity SaaS that survives without AI, so it is 0A.

name: Vevo Therapeutics
draft_rationale: Vevo Therapeutics (rebranded Tahoe) generates gigascale perturbative single-cell datasets and trains 'virtual cell' foundation models on them to discover precision cancer drugs, having open-sourced Tahoe-100M and partnered with NVIDIA/Arc Institute on biological foundation models. This is applied vertical AI where proprietary data and self-trained domain foundation models are the core mechanism (the taxonomy routes own foundation models for one vertical to 1E, not 1A). RAD-L because it builds its own models on a proprietary data flywheel with a deep research team, structurally independent of third-party GenAI APIs.
ambiguity_flag: 1A is the close alternative because the virtual-cell foundation models double as infrastructure others build on; I chose 1E per the single-vertical rule. Rebrand (Vevo->Tahoe) noted.

name: VideoGen
draft_rationale: VideoGen turns text/ideas into finished videos with AI: AI voiceovers (200+ voices, 50+ languages), AI avatars, generative images, AI B-roll, and text-to-video workflows, so the deliverable is generated media, which is 1G. RAD-H because it is a thin generative-media wrapper (tiny team, $500K funding) aggregating third-party generative models under an editor, exactly the profile the taxonomy assigns RAD-H.

name: Careerflow.ai
draft_rationale: Careerflow is a job-search platform combining a conventional core (job/application tracker, networking CRM, LinkedIn profile scoring, resume templates, browser extension) with many AI writing helpers (resume bullets, cover letters, LinkedIn posts, mock interviews). Stripping the AI still leaves a functional job-search management product, so AI augments a pre-existing platform, the 0B pattern, and the small $800K team shows no proprietary AI depth.
ambiguity_flag: 1C is a close call because most flagship features are LLM-generation wrappers; it hinges on whether the tracker/CRM core is substantial enough to be the real product.

name: Treat AI
draft_rationale: The Crunchbase copy ('AI-generated personalized photo products') is stale; the Website Evidence shows the current product, Typeface, an enterprise generative-AI marketing platform that produces on-brand images, copy, video, ads, and emails via an Arc brand-context graph and specialized content agents. The deliverable is generated marketing content, which the 'what is produced' rule assigns to 1G. RAD-M because it layers substantial proprietary depth (brand-context graph, enterprise workflow, governance, agent studio) on top of foundation models it still depends on.
ambiguity_flag: Pivot from Treat/photo products to Typeface noted; 1F (agentic) and 1D (thick integrator) are close, but the core deliverable is generated media, so 1G governs the subclass.

name: SheMate
draft_rationale: SheMate connects women athletes with youth sports through virtual mentoring and wellness groups facilitated by real women in sport, plus free video content and team/brand partnerships. The 'Virtual Assistant' keyword is misleading; the deliverable is human mentoring and community services, with no software product and no AI, which is 0C.

name: Blue Agilis
draft_rationale: Blue Agilis sells a healthcare value-based-care workflow-improvement platform (proprietary assessment tool, 'Smart Assist' workflow generation, real-time trend analytics) plus consulting/staff augmentation. The 'AI-enabled' language reads as marketing over a workflow-methodology and analytics product whose core value clearly survives without AI, so under the marketing-vs-mechanism guardrail it is conventional health-tech SaaS, 0A.
ambiguity_flag: 0B is a close call if 'Smart Assist' is a genuine shipped AI feature rather than branding.

name: AWKNG TV
draft_rationale: Website shows AWKNG TV operating as an IPTV subscription service reselling access to 24,000+ live channels and 100,000+ VOD titles, with setup tutorials, reseller plans, and 24/7 support. This is a consumer content-delivery/subscription service, not a software product the company builds and sells, and there is no AI anywhere in the mechanism. The Crunchbase description (Christian streaming TV) differs from the generic IPTV reseller shown on the site; the website product governs.
ambiguity_flag: 0A is the plausible alternative if the IPTV streaming platform is treated as the company's own software product, but the evidence reads as a subscription reseller of channel access rather than proprietary software.

name: AppMaster
draft_rationale: AppMaster sells a no-code platform that generates Go/Vue3 applications from visual drag-and-drop designs — a conventional code-generation mechanism, with pricing, docs, and FAQ pages describing visual programming, backend generation, and source-code export, and no LLM or ML mechanism shown anywhere. The short description's 'AI-generated source code' claim is not substantiated by any AI mechanism in the website evidence; the guardrail treats no-code codegen as a conventional product. The product is clearly software, so 0A rather than 0C.
ambiguity_flag: The description's 'AI-generated source code' phrasing makes 1C/0B superficially arguable, but the site shows deterministic template-based code generation with no AI feature, so 0A.

name: Unicodez Inc.
draft_rationale: Unicodez is an IT consulting, custom software development, and US IT staffing firm selling dedicated teams, engagement models (fixed-price, time & materials), and consulting services across AI/ML, mobile, cloud, and blockchain. Despite pervasive 'AI-First' branding, the deliverable is professional services (client projects and staff augmentation), not a software product of its own, so it is a services business (0C).

name: Personal AI
draft_rationale: Personal AI's current product is a memory-and-context infrastructure stack for AI systems: Memory Core (a unified memory service for self-improving AI sold to developers and AI engineers), Persona Studio (a no-code platform for building AI personas), and a My AI application on top. The primary customer is AI builders integrating persistent memory into agents and assistants, which would not exist without the AI ecosystem — squarely 1B. RAD-M because the company shows real proprietary depth (its own memory architecture and encoding/retrieval pipeline, NVIDIA/edge partnerships, SLM focus, pre-2023 founding) while the surrounding AI still depends on foundation models.
ambiguity_flag: 1F is a close alternative since the marketing describes agentic 'AI teammates', but the mechanism actually sold is memory/persona infrastructure for building AI, which fits 1B better.

name: Relvy AI
draft_rationale: Relvy's product is an AI agent that autonomously investigates on-call alerts: it plans multi-step investigations, queries logs/metrics/traces/code via purpose-built tools, follows runbooks, and produces auditable investigation notebooks — concrete autonomous multi-step tool use, not a chatbot. Removing the LLM breaks the product entirely, and the evidence (70% of alerts resolved autonomously in under 5 minutes) shows real agentic execution rather than 'agent' marketing. RAD-M because the core reasoning runs on third-party LLMs (BYO LLM keys, tuning Claude's RCA accuracy) but the company has built genuine proprietary depth: a knowledge graph, telemetry-scale tooling, and investigation memory.
ambiguity_flag: 1D is a plausible alternative since the agent investigates and reports rather than remediating systems, but autonomous multi-step tool-driven execution is the core mechanism, favoring 1F.

name: Innotreat
draft_rationale: Innotreat develops and sells physical chemical cleaning agents, degreasers, and water-treatment products based on patented 'free-radical' chemistry, sold into oil & gas, cooling towers, and commercial cleaning. There is no software product and no AI anywhere in the evidence; this is a physical-product chemical technology company, so 0C.

name: LiveFlow
draft_rationale: LiveFlow sells finance software whose core value is conventional: an FP&A platform syncing accounting data to Excel/Google Sheets for consolidation, reporting, and budgeting, plus the Flow ERP for multi-entity accounting and month-end close. The 'AI-native ERP' branding is backed by genuine but feature-level AI (automatic transaction categorization, bank reconciliation, dynamic close checklists, logged agents) layered on an ERP/FP&A product that would still deliver its value proposition without AI. That is the conventional-SaaS-plus-AI-feature pattern, so 0B rather than an AI-native subclass.
ambiguity_flag: 1D is arguable if Flow's AI agents are seen as the ERP's core mechanism, but the FP&A/ERP workflow (data sync, consolidation, reporting) predates and survives without AI, and coin flips go to 0.

name: Kastenberry
draft_rationale: Kastenberry is a merchant-services company — a registered ISO/MSP of Woodforest National Bank — selling credit card processing and reselling third-party POS systems through local sales reps, with the website dominated by rep testimonials and support claims. It does not build software of its own; the mechanism is a sales-and-service payments brokerage, so 0C with no AI anywhere in the evidence.

name: Immobileyes
draft_rationale: Immobileyes develops physical light-based non-lethal defense hardware: the HALAR flashing-light tactical flashlight and BLITZ diversionary device, and the Laser Storm counter-UAS laser sensor-jamming system. These are optics/photonics hardware products for law enforcement and the military, with no software product and no AI mechanism in the evidence, so 0C.

name: Positron
draft_rationale: Positron builds purpose-built LLM inference hardware — the Atlas appliance, Asimov custom silicon, and Titan systems — sold to clouds, AI companies, and enterprises running Transformer models. This is foundation-layer AI infrastructure: the artifact is the compute layer other AI products run on, and the company would not exist without AI as a workload. RAD-L because Positron designs its own silicon and systems and does not depend on third-party GenAI APIs; its dependency is on the AI ecosystem's demand, not on any model provider.

name: TIFIN Give
draft_rationale: TIFIN Give sells a white-label donor-advised-fund platform for wealth managers and broker-dealers: account opening, grant processing, multi-custody investments, tax-gain harvesting, and workplace giving. The core mechanism is conventional fintech workflow software; the 'AI agents' section describes a genuine conversational-AI feature for donors to explore causes and take actions, but the platform's value (DAF administration and asset retention) survives entirely without it. That is a real AI feature on a conventional product, so 0B.
ambiguity_flag: 0A is a close alternative if the conversational AI feature is judged as marketing-level rather than a genuine shipped feature; the detailed feature description tips it to 0B.

name: More Staffing
draft_rationale: More Staffing is an offshore recruitment and managed-staffing agency placing Filipino virtual professionals into US companies, wrapped in SOPs, coaching, and fractional leadership services. The deliverable is people and managed services, not a software product; the 'AI agents' page describes beta automations that assist the placed staff, an add-on to a services business where humans remain the core mechanism. Services business → 0C.

name: Swarm Aero
draft_rationale: Swarm Aero builds large UAV swarms for defense whose stated core capability is mission-level autonomy: the system translates user intent into vehicle-level behaviors that update in real time so small teams can mobilize thousands of uncrewed vehicles in communications-limited, contested environments. That value proposition is impossible without AI/autonomy — a human crew cannot pilot thousands of vehicles — making autonomy the product engine, purpose-built for the defense vertical (1E). RAD-L because the autonomy stack is proprietary robotics/CV-style AI with no dependence on third-party GenAI APIs.
ambiguity_flag: A defensible alternative is 0A/0B treating Swarm as primarily an aircraft manufacturer (much evidence is about airframes and manufacturing) with autonomy as a feature; the swarm-autonomy-centric value proposition tips it to 1E.

name: Prime Intellect
draft_rationale: Prime Intellect builds the base of the AI stack: a distributed GPU compute platform (on-demand and multi-node clusters), hosted RL training infrastructure, RL environments hub, sandboxes, and open frameworks (PRIME-RL, Verifiers) — and it trains its own frontier models (INTELLECT-2, INTELLECT-3, a 100B+ MoE). The customers are AI builders and the artifacts are models and training/compute infrastructure, squarely 1A. RAD-L because the company trains its own models and operates its own training/inference stack rather than depending on third-party GenAI APIs.

name: FireFold Distribution
draft_rationale: FireFold Distribution is an IT hardware distributor sourcing networking, fiber optic, and electronic products for resellers, positioning itself as an extension of clients' purchasing departments. It sells physical goods and sourcing services, has no software product, and shows no AI anywhere in the evidence, so 0C.

name: NoteGoat
draft_rationale: NoteGoat's entire product is AI-generated notes, summaries, and analyses from live sessions, uploaded documents, emails, and YouTube — a self-serve consumer/prosumer subscription where the LLM call IS the mechanism; removing it leaves nothing. The evidence shows no proprietary models, no fine-tuning claims, no data flywheel, and no enterprise workflow depth — the commodity summarization thin-wrapper pattern. RAD-H because the product is structurally dependent on foundation-model APIs with no visible proprietary defensibility.

name: Hitech Health Solutions
draft_rationale: Hitech Health Solutions is a small healthcare IT consulting and custom software development shop: services pages emphasize team-on-demand staffing for transitions, EHR upgrade/migration services, project management, and healthcare consulting. The remote-patient-monitoring 'platform' copy is generic and reads as custom solutions delivered as services rather than a productized software offering, and there is no AI in the mechanism. A services/consulting business → 0C.
ambiguity_flag: 0A is plausible if the RPM/RCM 'platform' is a real productized software offering, but the dominant evidence is consulting, staffing, and project-management services.

name: Insights First
draft_rationale: Insights First is a data-driven marketing and market-research agency delivering segmentation studies, brand positioning, go-to-market planning, and in-market testing as consulting engagements, leveraging third-party research partners (Dynata, M3, dscout). The deliverable is research and strategy services, not a software product, and no AI appears in the mechanism, so 0C.

name: Kazm
draft_rationale: The website (Lightning Rod) shows a completely different product from the Crunchbase web3-loyalty description — a pivot, so the website product governs. Lightning Rod is a pipeline/API that turns raw documents and public sources into verified training datasets ('Future-as-Label' methodology) and trains compact domain-expert models; customers are teams fine-tuning and evaluating AI, i.e., AI builders — synthetic/verified training-data generation is squarely 1B. RAD-M because the company demonstrates proprietary depth (novel labeling methodology, its own frontier-beating Foresight-32B model) while the generation pipeline still leans on the LLM ecosystem.
ambiguity_flag: Clear pivot: Crunchbase describes web3 loyalty (Kazm) while the site sells an AI training-data platform (Lightning Rod); classified on the website product per the pivot rule.

name: Martian
draft_rationale: Martian builds LLM routing infrastructure (route every prompt to the best model, RouterBench, judge aggregation, Airlock compliance) commercialized from its mechanistic-interpretability research; its customers are teams building on LLMs, and the product category would not exist without the AI ecosystem — 1B. RAD-M rather than RAD-H because there are strong proprietary-depth signals: an in-house research team from DeepMind/Anthropic/Meta, novel interpretability and model-mapping methods, and multi-provider routing that reduces dependence on any single model API, even though the business remains structurally tied to third-party LLMs.
ambiguity_flag: 1A is a close alternative given the core AI research focus (interpretability, model mapping), but the commercialized artifact is routing/tooling atop others' models rather than the foundation layer itself.

name: Enjoy Ceres
draft_rationale: Enjoy Ceres makes high-protein plant-based snacks — a physical consumer food product per the descriptions and keywords. The scraped website evidence contains only unrelated wellness-blog filler (the domain appears repurposed or parked), showing no software or AI mechanism of any kind. A food/physical-goods company → 0C.
ambiguity_flag: The website evidence is unrelated blog content that does not match the company, so the classification rests on the Crunchbase descriptions; the alternative would be the insufficient-information 0A fallback.

name: Autom Mate
draft_rationale: Autom Mate sells a no-code/low-code hyperautomation platform combining iPaaS, RPA, and BPA — integrating and automating tools like Jira, ServiceNow, and Teams for IT service delivery. That core integration/workflow mechanism predates and survives without AI; the Agent Composer for building AI agents is a genuine but additional module layered onto the orchestration platform. This is the conventional-automation-SaaS-plus-agent-feature pattern the guardrail assigns to 0B, not 1F/1B.
ambiguity_flag: 1F is arguable given the AI Agent Composer product line, but the platform's primary mechanism is conventional iPaaS/RPA orchestration with agents as an added capability, so 0B.

name: Detecteam
draft_rationale: Detecteam REFLEX is a detection-engineering and validation platform: a scenario library of adversary TTPs, synthetic attack-data generation matched to the customer's SIEM/XDR, detection coverage metrics, and detection-as-code lifecycle automation — a mechanism that works as conventional security tooling. AI appears as feature-level claims ('AI-powered Adversary Behavior Builder', 'AI-powered insights', 'AI-native pipeline') augmenting that workflow rather than demonstrably being the engine. Since the core detection-validation value survives without AI and the AI claims are mostly assertions, the coin-flip rule sends this to 0B.
ambiguity_flag: 1E is the plausible alternative if the AI-driven detection-generation pipeline is truly the core mechanism, but the evidence shows a conventional detection library/validation workflow with AI framed as an enhancing layer.

name: Blank Dental
draft_rationale: Blank Dental is a multi-location dental practice offering implants, cosmetic dentistry, and emergency care in Ohio. It is a clinical services business; the advanced equipment mentioned (3D printers, CBCT, milling machines) is tooling for delivering care, not a software product, and there is no AI mechanism. Services/clinic → 0C.

name: Decart
draft_rationale: Decart is a frontier AI research lab training its own foundational generative interactive models (Oasis real-time playable world model, MirageLSD live-stream diffusion, Lucy real-time video editing) and selling systems-level AI infrastructure (Decart Optimization Stack) to AI labs, clouds, and chip makers. The artifacts are models and the infrastructure other AI products depend on — foundation layer, 1A. RAD-L because Decart trains proprietary models and builds its own optimization/inference stack with no dependence on third-party GenAI APIs.

name: AnyLawyer
draft_rationale: AnyLawyer sells an LLM-based legal document intelligence platform for Polish lawyers: upload case documents, ask questions, get answers with clickable source citations, adjustable AI reasoning depth, and built-in retrieval over Polish legal sources (Dziennik Ustaw, Monitor Polski, EU regulations). Removing the LLM breaks the entire product, so it is AI-native. The curated Polish-law source integration, citation/traceability system, OCR-plus-indexing pipeline, and team/org workflow features are proprietary depth beyond a bare API call, making it a thick integrator (1D) rather than thin wrapper; there is no evidence of proprietary model training (explicit zero-training policy), so third-party model dependence stays material — RAD-M.
ambiguity_flag: Close call with 1E (vertical legal AI a la Harvey) and 1C: the domain corpus integration argues for 1E, but with no fine-tuning or proprietary model evidence and a 1-10 person team the wrapper-depth framing (1D) fits better.

name: Emerge and Rise
draft_rationale: Emerge and Rise is a San Antonio venture development organization delivering 1:1 advisory, consulting, workshops, B2B matchmaking, and grant/funding guidance to small businesses, priced at $125/hour after free consultation. It sells human-delivered services, not a software product, and the only AI content is blog commentary about AI adoption. Services business with an ordinary website is 0C.

name: VSOptima
draft_rationale: VSOptima sells a SaaS operations digital-twin platform: model business processes, run scenario simulations, and optimize staffing, inventory, and scheduling decisions. The core mechanism is simulation modeling and analytics — a conventional operations-research software pattern that works without AI. 'AI-powered insights' and a passing Generative AI mention on the about page are marketing framing over a simulation product, so 0A rather than an AI-native class.
ambiguity_flag: 0B is plausible if the predictive-analytics/auto-decision features are genuinely ML-driven, but the evidence shows no concrete shipped AI feature beyond taglines, so it stays 0A.

name: LifeMedz
draft_rationale: LifeMedz is a wholesale distributor/supply-chain firm for industrial and medical disposables — gloves, masks, PPE, paper products, trash liners, building supplies — with an ecommerce storefront. It sells physical goods and procurement/logistics services, not software, and there is no AI anywhere in the product. Clear 0C.

name: World Franchise Group
draft_rationale: World Franchise Group is a franchise and real estate consulting firm: human consultants match candidates to 400+ franchises, guide the purchase process, and earn referral fees from franchisors. The mentioned 'FranTech 360' platform page is a 404 and appears to be an internal transaction-tracking tool, not a software product they sell. A professional-services business delivered through a website is 0C.

name: ChipEleven
draft_rationale: ChipEleven is a one-person open-source semiconductor effort delivering 'fully open commercial-grade silicon' (a quad-core open POWER SoC) plus hobbyist tooling like a Rust VCD waveform parser. The deliverable is hardware/silicon designs, not a software product for sale, and there is no AI mechanism anywhere in the evidence. Physical/hardware product company → 0C.
ambiguity_flag: 0A is a plausible alternative since open-source chip design and the FastWave tooling are arguably technology/software artifacts, but the stated deliverable is silicon hardware, so 0C.

name: Tixbase
draft_rationale: Tixbase sells an integrated event-ticketing suite: branded storefront, mobile app, point of sale, access-control scanning, fan-to-fan resale, and marketing campaigns — a product category that fully predates GenAI and retains its value without AI. 'Tixy', the AI agent that creates events, adjusts pricing, and builds fan segments from a conversation, is a genuine shipped assistant layered on that conventional ticketing core, which is the classic SaaS-plus-copilot pattern → 0B.
ambiguity_flag: The site brands itself 'The AI Platform for Live Events' and Tixy could suggest 1F/1D, but the mechanism shown is a ticketing platform with an assistant bolted on, so 0B per the not-1F gate.

name: Tensorpunk
draft_rationale: Tensorpunk's product MACE is a generative diffusion sampler VST/AU plugin: text prompt in, newly generated audio out, running Stable Audio Open locally on the user's GPU. The deliverable is generated audio content that cannot exist without generative models — squarely 1G. RAD-M because the architecture is API-independent (fully local, no cloud) and the company is building its own diffusion fine-tuning trainer (ANVIL 2, with a pre-2023 GAN-based ANVIL 1 lineage), but the core generation still rests on a third-party open foundation model rather than models trained from scratch.

name: YearOne
draft_rationale: The website shows a pivot from the Crunchbase description (code-school hiring support) to a 'software engineering intelligence platform' — analytics over commits, PRs, tickets, and workload to find delivery bottlenecks; classifying the website product per the pivot rule. The case studies describe conventional metrics analysis (review-load concentration, rework, commit volume), and the 'AI Guided Assistance / AI Intelligence' items are feature labels on a partially lorem-ipsum placeholder site with no demonstrated AI mechanism. Marketing-led 'Powered by AI' over a conventional analytics product → 0A.
ambiguity_flag: 0B is a close alternative if the AI-guided-insights features are genuinely shipped; the thin, placeholder-heavy evidence made mechanism verification impossible, so classified conservatively as 0A.

name: Maxafi Holdings
draft_rationale: Maxafi sells fintech infrastructure: merchant lifecycle management for ISOs, payment processing and routing, tax settlement, embedded lending, wallets, and a merchant API — a payments platform whose core value is entirely non-AI. It ships genuine AI features on top: an AI agent in the API/onboarding flow (e.g. connecting Shopify by chat), AI credit decisioning, and the licensed Fiskl autonomous AI accounting module. Payments remain the mechanism customers buy, so AI-augmented 0B rather than AI-native.

name: Facets.cloud
draft_rationale: Facets.cloud is a platform-engineering orchestration product (founded 2021): Terraform-native infrastructure provisioning, environment automation, multi-cloud governance, and self-service deployments — a conventional DevOps/IaC platform that predates and survives without AI. The Kubernetes Agent for natural-language debugging and the 'Facets Intelligence' AI assistant are genuine shipped features added onto that core, matching the SaaS-plus-AI-copilot pattern → 0B.
ambiguity_flag: Marketing leads with 'AI-native orchestration' and roadmap agents could suggest 1B/1F, but the product's primary purpose is infrastructure orchestration for engineering teams with AI bolted on, so 0B per the not-1B gate.

name: Yay! Forms
draft_rationale: Yay! Forms is a form/survey builder with logic branching, integrations, drop-off analytics, and per-submission pricing — a Typeform-style product whose core works and sells without AI. It ships real AI features on top: sentiment/emotion detection on responses, automatic keyword extraction, and AI-generated feedback emails driven by user prompts. Customers still buy it as a form builder, so AI is a meaningful add-on layer, not the engine → 0B.
ambiguity_flag: 1C is the plausible alternative if the AI-insights layer is judged the core value proposition ('let AI turn your data into insights'), but the shown mechanism is form building and data collection, so 0B.

name: Rowan
draft_rationale: Rowan sells clean-ingredient dog shampoo, coat care products, and gift bundles through an ecommerce storefront. The product is physical consumer goods with no software and no AI anywhere in the evidence. Clear 0C.

name: Fierce
draft_rationale: The website shows Fierce has become GalaxyOne (Galaxy Digital's consumer arm), a multi-asset investing app: FDIC-insured high-yield cash, crypto trading and staking, commission-free brokerage, and stock lending — classified per the current site. The product is a self-serve fintech software platform; the only AI mention is corporate boilerplate about Galaxy's data-center business. No AI in the product mechanism → 0A.
ambiguity_flag: 0C is arguable because the deliverable is regulated financial services (banking/brokerage), but the company's own product is the consumer investing software platform, which fits 0A fintech; note the Fierce→GalaxyOne rebrand vs the Crunchbase description.

name: Bitskout
draft_rationale: Bitskout's product is AI document extraction: users give a few example documents and the AI learns to pull fields from invoices, POs, CVs, and emails, delivered as plugins into monday.com, Asana, Zapier, and Power Automate, plus an agent that answers questions over processed documents. Remove the AI extraction models and there is no product, so it is AI-native. The proprietary layer is modest — templates and integrations around third-party/hosted models, a 1-10 person team, ~$728k raised — placing it at 1C; RAD-M rather than RAD-H because example-based document AI predates the 2023 GenAI wave (2022 founding) and the enterprise tier mentions fine-tuning and swappable third-party models.
ambiguity_flag: 1D is a close alternative given the 40+ template library and multi-tool integration ecosystem, but the depth looks replicable by a small team, so thin wrapper 1C.

name: Postilize
draft_rationale: Postilize (Proactive) sells legal business-development software: Signals monitors leadership changes, M&A, and filings and matches scored alerts to firm relationships; Cleanse auto-syncs CRM contact data from email/calendar/LinkedIn; Chat is an explicitly Claude-powered assistant over the firm's relationship data. The core value — event monitoring, relationship management, and CRM hygiene for law-firm rainmakers — is an ERM/CRM workflow that exists without AI, with genuine AI features (Claude chat, signal scoring) layered on. That is the conventional-SaaS-plus-AI pattern → 0B.
ambiguity_flag: 1E/1D is a close call because the headline value is 'predicting client legal needs' and Chat is LLM-driven; if the prediction engine is genuinely ML-core rather than event filtering plus scoring, this would tip AI-native.

name: Four Points Health
draft_rationale: Four Points Health is an intelligent-automation consulting and deployment firm for healthcare: advisory, discovery-to-sustainment services, 60+ client deployments, and optional fully outsourced automation operations, built on RPA/AI technologies from partners. Despite heavy AI/ML language, the company sells professional services and systems integration, not its own software product → 0C.

name: Birdie Books & Cafe
draft_rationale: Birdie Books & Cafe is a physical independent bookshop, bar, and bakery in Westerville, Ohio, offering book clubs, a curated gift service, and ebooks/audiobooks referred through third-party partners (Bookshop.org, Libro.fm). Retail with no software product and no AI → 0C.

name: Doorvest
draft_rationale: Doorvest is a full-service single-family rental investing business: its team sources and underwrites homes, manages renovations through contractor networks, places tenants, and property-manages, with an online marketplace as the storefront. The core deliverable is a human/operations-heavy real estate service (buying and managing houses), not a software product, and there is no AI in the mechanism → 0C.
ambiguity_flag: 0A is a plausible alternative since the end-to-end platform and marketplace are software-mediated and the company is well funded ($92.5M), but the value delivered is real-estate transaction and management services, so 0C.

name: Vendia
draft_rationale: Vendia (founded 2020) sells a data-sharing/integration platform — smart APIs, databases, and distributed-ledger reconciliation used by Delta, BMW, and Fannie Mae for cross-partner data automation — a pre-GenAI product whose value is data integration, not AI. Its new MCP Gateway packages that same integration hub as an endpoint for AI agents, which is exactly the 'conventional iPaaS/data platform adds an MCP/for-AI-agents feature' pattern the 1B gate excludes → 0B.
ambiguity_flag: 1B is the plausible alternative since current marketing leads with MCP Gateway for AI agents, but the underlying product and go-to-market predate AI and the agent gateway is a new interface onto the existing data hub.

name: BasicDB
draft_rationale: Basic (BasicDB) sells open-source local-first data infrastructure: personal data stores with auth, sync, and SDKs for React/Next/Expo, positioned as a Supabase alternative where users own their data. The database/sync platform is the shipped product and works entirely without AI; agents are one entity type that can hold a PDS, and the 'Spaces' agentic interface is only in early access. Core mechanism is conventional data infrastructure → 0A.

name: Loomiti
draft_rationale: Loomiti is a small marketing agency offering SEO, social media marketing, marketing consulting, and graphic design services, centered on its founder's consulting work. It sells human-delivered services with no software product and no AI → 0C.

name: SuperDuperAi
draft_rationale: SuperDuperAI's flagship is an AI video generation platform — 'turn vibes into videos', multi-agent text/mood-to-video generation with LoRA-based consistent characters, sold to musicians, creators, and agencies — plus a secondary RAG chatbot product. The customer deliverable is generated video content that cannot be produced without generative models, so 1G. A solo-founder, March-2024 studio with no proprietary model training evidence is a thin layer over third-party generative models → RAD-H, per the thin-generative-wrapper rule.

name: International Directional Drilling
draft_rationale: International Directional Drilling is a horizontal directional drilling contractor installing underground pipes, conduits, fiber-optic cables, and utilities. It is a physical construction-services business with no software product and no AI → 0C.

name: BlueNote
draft_rationale: The website shows a pivot from the Crunchbase insurance-risk description to AI agents for life sciences documentation: LLM-based drafting of validation reports, CMC/safety documentation, protocols, and consent forms, with agent workflow builders, dataset search over ELN/QMS sources, and full source traceability — classifying the website product. The AI drafting/agents ARE the product, so it is AI-native; it is built on third-party LLMs (Anthropic case study, 'zero data retention policy with LLMs', explicit no-training-on-customer-data), with substantial proprietary depth in regulated-workflow integration, traceability, and on-premise/compliance features → thick integrator 1D, RAD-M given continuing third-party model dependence.
ambiguity_flag: 1E is a close alternative given the deep life-sciences vertical workflow, but with no proprietary model or fine-tuning evidence the LLM-wrapper rule points to 1D; also note the pivot from the insurance-focused Crunchbase description.

name: NeverEnding
draft_rationale: NeverEnding sells a web app for character creation, scene illustration, and webcomics, but the website evidence shows a conventional mechanism: a point-and-click character builder with preset body/clothing options, a drag-and-drop Scene Creator, and a library of 2,000+ pre-made assets. Despite the 'ethical AI tools' phrase in the short description, no page demonstrates generative AI as the output mechanism — users assemble pre-drawn assets manually. The counterfactual holds: remove AI and the product as shown is unchanged, so this is traditional creative software (0A).
ambiguity_flag: The Crunchbase description mentions 'ethical AI tools,' so 1G is the plausible alternative if generation actually powers the builder, but the site shows only manual asset assembly, so the marketing-vs-mechanism guardrail keeps it at 0A.

name: Sprinter Spirits
draft_rationale: Sprinter Spirits sells physical beverage products — vodka sodas and the k2o skin-hydration drink mix with collagen peptides and electrolytes — through a standard ecommerce storefront. There is no software product and no AI anywhere in the evidence; the store locator, subscriptions, and product pages are ordinary retail infrastructure. Physical consumer goods with an ordinary website is 0C.

name: Arcjet
draft_rationale: Arcjet sells a developer security SDK whose core capabilities — bot detection, rate limiting, email validation, attack protection, data redaction — are conventional application-security functions that exist independently of AI. The current site markets AI-focused security features (prompt injection detection, agent tool controls, AI data-loss prevention, token budget enforcement), which are genuine shipped features aimed at protecting AI workloads, but the product mechanism remains a security rules engine, not AI. Per the 'Not 1B' gate, a conventional security product that has added a for-AI-agents feature set is 0B, not AI-native infrastructure.
ambiguity_flag: 1B is the plausible alternative given how heavily the homepage leads with AI-workflow security, but the underlying product (security-as-code SDK with rate limiting and bot detection) predates and survives without the AI ecosystem, so the bolt-on gate points to 0B.

name: Planet
draft_rationale: Planet is a subscription content service: daily astrological forecasts written and delivered by text, with replies answered by in-house human astrologers and paid 1:1 astrologer appointments. The evidence shows the delivery runs on a third-party SMS-subscription platform (Subtext), and there is no software product of Planet's own and no AI mechanism anywhere. A human-expert content/services business delivered through texting is 0C.
ambiguity_flag: 0A is a plausible alternative if one treats the text-subscription offering as a software product, but the evidence shows human astrologers producing the content on rented messaging infrastructure, so it reads as a services/media business.

name: Intangible
draft_rationale: Intangible's deliverable is AI-generated images and video: users compose 3D scenes then generate renders and clips, with generative features metered by computational credits (image generation, video generation, LoRA styling). The FAQ calls it an 'AI-native 3D platform' and describes prompt-to-scene generation plus LoRA-based style consistency — without generative models the product produces no output, so generation is the engine (1G). RAD-M rather than RAD-H because there are real proprietary-depth signals: a claimed 'spatial intelligence model,' a proprietary 3D-scene layer that constrains generations to shot composition and CAD geometry, and a Pixar/Apple-alum team, even though the copy also references orchestrating 'the newest AI models' from third parties.
ambiguity_flag: RAD-H is a close alternative since the marketing admits reliance on the newest third-party generative models; the proprietary spatial/3D control layer is what tips this to RAD-M.

name: Voyage AI
draft_rationale: Voyage AI trains and sells its own embedding models and rerankers (voyage-3.5, voyage-4 series, rerank-2.5, domain models for code/finance/law) via API — the model itself is the artifact, sold to AI builders as a component of RAG stacks. This is base-of-the-AI-stack infrastructure for other AI products, built by a Stanford/MIT/Berkeley research team, which is squarely 1A. RAD-L because the company demonstrably trains proprietary state-of-the-art models rather than consuming third-party GenAI APIs.

name: Evolve Therapy Services
draft_rationale: Evolve Therapy Services is a therapy management and consulting firm for long-term-care operators: it transitions contract therapy teams in-house, provides Medicaid case-mix analysis, staff education, and compliance oversight. This is a professional-services business delivered by clinicians and operators; there is no software product and no AI in the evidence. Services businesses with an ordinary website are 0C.

name: Marker Learning
draft_rationale: Marker's product is an AI report writer for special-education evaluations: it ingests referral and assessment documents and instantly drafts full psychoeducational reports with per-sentence citations, built-in compliance checks, and case management — remove the AI drafting and the product's core value disappears. It is purpose-built vertical AI for one domain (school psychology), with clinical experts (Chief Clinical Officer, R&D VP) embedding domain standards into the pipeline, closer to the DeepScribe/Abridge pattern than a generic wrapper. RAD-M because the domain workflow, citation system, and assessment-battery depth show real proprietary investment, though there is no evidence of proprietary model training so third-party LLM dependence likely remains material. Note: the Crunchbase description (evaluation services) lags the website, which now sells the report-writing platform.
ambiguity_flag: 1D is a close alternative since the intelligence likely flows from third-party LLM APIs with proprietary workflow on top; the depth of domain-specific compliance, citation, and assessment coverage tips it to 1E.

name: Harmonate
draft_rationale: Harmonate sells a data-operations automation platform for fund administration and finance: extraction and normalization of data from multiple sources, reconciliation, approval workflows, and reporting. The 'AI and advanced machine learning' language is marketing framing around what the pages actually describe — workflow automation, one-click transaction creation, and real-time reconciliation — and the company's own timeline shows the platform ran 2020-2024 with AI-driven automation only 'unveiled' in the 2025 2.0 release. The core product mechanism survives with AI removed, so this is traditional fintech SaaS (0A).
ambiguity_flag: 0B is plausible if the document extraction/normalization is genuinely ML-powered, but the evidence shows only slogan-level AI claims over a conventional automation mechanism, so the marketing-vs-mechanism guardrail keeps it 0A.

name: Melrose Park Logistics
draft_rationale: Melrose Park Logistics is an Amazon Delivery Service Partner running courier operations in the Chicagoland area; the single-page website advertises delivery services and driver hiring. This is a physical logistics services business with no software product and no AI. Clear 0C.

name: Zyphra
draft_rationale: Zyphra trains its own open foundation models (ZAYA language models, Zonos TTS, ZUNA BCI model, Zamba series), publishes novel architecture research (Compressed Convolutional Attention, Tree Attention, hybrid SSM/transformer training), builds trillion-token datasets (Zyda), and runs an inference cloud serving frontier open-weight models to developers and hyperscalers. The artifact is the model layer and the compute/inference infrastructure other AI builders use — the definition of 1A. RAD-L because the company trains and serves its own models end-to-end (including on AMD hardware it co-optimizes) rather than depending on third-party GenAI APIs.

name: Querri
draft_rationale: Querri's product is a plain-language AI data analyst: users ask questions and the system cleans, transforms, analyzes, and explains their data — removing the LLM removes the product's entire value proposition, so it is AI-native. The evidence shows a small team ($460K funding), no proprietary models, no fine-tuning claims, and a use-case pattern (NL-to-analysis over uploaded business data) that is replicable atop the same LLM APIs, making it a thin wrapper (1C). RAD-H follows from GENAI-ERA founding (March 2023), no proprietary depth signals, and structural dependence on frontier LLM capability.
ambiguity_flag: 0B is the plausible alternative if the drag-and-drop dashboards are seen as the core with AI assist on top, but the entire pitch and testimonials center on the ask-in-plain-language analyst, so AI is the mechanism.

name: FORAY bioscience
draft_rationale: FORAY is a biotechnology/biomanufacturing company: it grows plant products and seeds from single cells for foods, materials, and reforestation — a physical-goods science business, not a software vendor. The Pando 'AI platform for Plant Science' with predictive models appears as an internal accelerant and demo-able tool within the biomanufacturing offering, staffed by a single AI/ML fellow on a team of wet-lab scientists. The company's deliverable is biology, not software or AI, so 0C.
ambiguity_flag: The Pando AI platform (predictive models that 'double success rates') makes 0B or even 1E arguable if Pando is sold as a standalone product, but the evidence positions it as part of a physical biomanufacturing business, keeping this 0C.

name: Yodayo
draft_rationale: Yodayo's product is generated anime content: AI image generation, video generation, music and voice generation, plus LLM-powered character roleplay — the deliverable to users is generated media and conversation, impossible without generative models (1G). The stack openly names third-party models (GLM, DeepSeek, Claude for chat; Minimax, Veo 3, Kling for video; Stable Diffusion, Flux, Illustrious-XL for images). RAD-M rather than RAD-H because the platform hosts a 105,000+ community model/LoRA hub with in-house LoRA training and self-hostable open-weight image models, giving credible partial independence from any single vendor API, and the company predates the 2023 GenAI wave (founded 2021).
ambiguity_flag: RAD-H is a close alternative since chat and video depend on third-party frontier APIs (Claude, Veo, Kling); the open-weight model hub and LoRA training infrastructure are what tip it to RAD-M.

name: Groundworks Brazilian Jiu Jitsu
draft_rationale: Groundworks is a Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu gym in Bethesda offering adult and kids martial-arts classes with monthly memberships. It is a local in-person services business; the website is informational with third-party signup links, and there is no software product or AI. Clear 0C.

name: Saphira AI
draft_rationale: Saphira's product is AI-generated safety-compliance engineering: it generates HARAs, TARAs, FMEAs, and full safety cases from system design documents, runs a standards chatbot, and performs gap analysis against ISO 26262/10218/13482 — the generation and document-understanding capability IS the product, and the security page confirms LLMs and embedding models power it with human-in-the-loop hallucination review. It is purpose-built for one vertical (hardware safety certification) with deep workflow embedding (traceability into SysML/CAD/FEA, TÜV/UL/CE-ready outputs), fitting 1E. RAD-M because the architecture uses open-source LLMs deployable fully on-premises with no central fine-tuning, reducing dependence on any single vendor API even though GenAI capability remains structurally required.
ambiguity_flag: 1D is a close alternative since the intelligence comes from (open-source) LLMs rather than proprietary trained models; the vertical workflow depth and domain-specific safety pipelines tip it to 1E.

name: LeadCRM
draft_rationale: LeadCRM is a Chrome extension for LinkedIn-to-CRM sync: one-click contact capture, bi-directional sync, waterfall data enrichment from a 700M contact database, org charts, and deal management — a data-plumbing product whose core value survives entirely without AI. It ships genuine, visible AI features (AI comment generator, AI response assistant, AI intent-surge detection) that it markets as differentiators, but these are add-ons atop the sync/enrichment core. Conventional sales SaaS plus bolted-on AI assist is the canonical 0B pattern.

name: Mulltiply
draft_rationale: Mulltiply's headline product is an AI WhatsApp ordering bot: it parses free-form text, pictures, and voice messages from retailers and converts them into confirmed ERP orders 'with 99% accuracy using advanced NLP' — remove the AI message-understanding and the product collapses to manual order entry. The evidence shows a tiny team (1-10), no proprietary model claims, and a mechanism (multimodal message parsing plus catalog matching) that rides on third-party LLM/NLP capability, making it a thin wrapper (1C). RAD-H because there are no proprietary-depth signals and the multimodal understanding is structurally tied to current-generation model APIs.
ambiguity_flag: 0B is the plausible alternative if the Mulltiply ERP is viewed as the core product with the bot as a feature, but the entire site sells the AI ordering bot with the ERP as backing, so AI is the mechanism.

name: VRenity, Inc.
draft_rationale: VRenity's current offering is 'Synthetic Reality' — fully AI-generated worlds, avatars, and cinematic content for music videos, commercials, podcasts, and VR experiences, plus an AI tutor character for classrooms; the deliverable to every customer is generated media that could not exist without generative models (1G). Note the pivot: Crunchbase describes a VR mindfulness product, but the website now centers AI-generated film/VR production, so I classify the website product. RAD-H because it is a tiny team ($400K, 1-10 people) with no proprietary model claims, structurally dependent on third-party generative tooling for its core output.
ambiguity_flag: 0C is a plausible alternative reading since much of the offering is bespoke production services (music videos, commercials) rather than a self-serve product, but the mechanism producing the deliverable is generative AI, which the taxonomy treats as the engine.

name: Colorsmith
draft_rationale: Colorsmith sells physical custom hair-color kits and hair-care products for men through an ecommerce site; formulas are individually crafted by licensed human colorists based on a customer profile and photo, then shipped in a box. The deliverable is a physical consumer product plus human colorist service — no software product, no AI mechanism. Clear 0C.

name: Hounen Solar
draft_rationale: Hounen Solar manufactures solar modules (monocrystalline PERC and N-TYPE panels) at a South Carolina facility for residential, commercial, and utility-scale installations. The evidence is entirely physical-product manufacturing — datasheets, module efficiencies, factory news — with no software product and no AI. Clear 0C.

name: Tildei
draft_rationale: Tildei sells AI agents that run booking operations end-to-end for salons and family entertainment centers: the Booking Agent checks availability, confirms appointments, updates the customer's booking systems, manages reschedules 'without your team stepping in,' and the Retention Agent proactively initiates outreach to fill off-peak slots and rescue cancellations. This is concrete autonomous multi-step execution — the agents act on external systems and initiate contact, not just answer chat — clearing the 'Not 1F' gate (their own FAQ distinguishes it from a chatbot: 'Tildei completes the booking'). RAD-H because the conversational intelligence shows no proprietary model evidence — agents are 'trained on your brand voice' via configuration and guardrails atop what is structurally third-party LLM capability, with a small team and $6M raised.
ambiguity_flag: 1D is a close alternative (thick integrator: deep Zenoti/Boulevard/Acuity integrations and managed-service depth atop LLM APIs), but the product's core is agents autonomously executing bookings and outreach, which fits 1F better.

name: Steel Aerospace Group
draft_rationale: The website (operating as ResCon) sells ultra-efficient edge machine-learning: custom low-latency ML models for aerospace/IoT/wearables clients and the BareML SDK, a toolkit that lets engineers generate and train their own efficient embedded models — a product that exists only because ML development exists, with AI builders/engineers as the customer (1B). Depth is demonstrably proprietary and classical: reservoir-computing-based ReACH software validated in a NASA Space Act Agreement, MLPerf Tiny benchmarking, and on-device inference with 'no cloud, no massive data centers.' RAD-L because the company builds and trains its own models with an API-independent, pre-GenAI classical-ML architecture — a third-party GenAI API shutdown would not affect the product. Note the mismatch: Crunchbase describes 'defense and space manufacturing,' but the website shows the edge-ML product, which I classify.
ambiguity_flag: 1E is a plausible alternative reading (applied ML solutions delivered vertically to aerospace/IoT clients), but the SDK-centric vision of enabling engineers to build their own models fits AI-development tooling (1B) better.

name: Titan Lending
draft_rationale: Titan Lending is a non-bank mortgage lender offering residential, commercial, agricultural, and fix-and-flip loans, with an NMLS license and a human loan team guiding borrowers through appraisal and closing. Generic references to 'top-of-the-line lending technology' and mortgage calculators are ordinary business tooling, not a software product of its own. A lending services business is 0C.

name: Rush Roto
draft_rationale: Rush Roto's app generates professional product photos and videos for small businesses: the headline is 'Instant A.I. Photos and videos in seconds,' and every pricing tier is metered in AI image generations (30 free, 1,000, unlimited) plus AI templates and background removal. The deliverable is AI-generated product imagery — without generative models the product cannot produce its core output, so 1G. RAD-H because it is a tiny team ($565K raised) with no proprietary model claims, and generative-media wrappers with thin proprietary layers default to high structural dependence on external model capability.
ambiguity_flag: 0B is a plausible alternative if the video editor and designer templates are seen as the core product with AI generation as a feature, but the generation credits are the product's central metered value, so generation is the engine.
